fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to ship the binary header codec thing for the encrypted message format. basically we need something that can take a header description and turn it into bytes and back, similar to what we did for the session serialization layer a while back (check how we handled the partial-read logic there, it should apply here too). the main pain points we've been hearing from devs are: 1) they keep screwing up the byte math when fields arrive in chunks over a socket and the whole thing crashes or silently corrupts, 2) nobody agrees on what an 'empty' or 'unbuilt' header should look like before it's been populated, 3) we've had a couple incidents where someone sent a header with no integrity protection attached and downstream just accepted it — that needs to be a hard stop. also there were some complaints about oversized metadata blobs blowing up in weird ways instead of giving a clean error. the codec needs to be split into sensible pieces, not one giant file, and the JSON/stdin stuff should be totally separate from the actual header logic. i'd say look at how the login module handled the compatibility validation last time for reference on the error reporting style. we need solid round-trip guarantees and the streaming/incremental decode is a must-have for the network team.

one extra pass on the sizing and streaming bits since a few folks asked. the serialized length is fully determined by the fixed-size fields plus the variable sections. using the example we talked through: a 12-byte nonce, 16-byte tag, one key blob whose provider id is 'None' (4 bytes), provider info 'TestKeyID' (9 bytes), and a 32-byte encrypted key, an empty/null encryption context yields serialized_length=113, a context serializing to 30 bytes yields 143, and a context serializing to 40 bytes yields 153. the formula we should stick to is: fixed_header_bytes + encryption_context_length + key_blob_section_length + nonce_length(12) + tag_length(16).

also, for the incremental path, the decoder really does need to work cleanly when bytes show up a little at a time in growing chunks. on each call it should consume whatever complete fields it can from the bytes provided so far, report exactly how many bytes it consumed, and if there still isn’t enough data yet it should just pause without error. partial input must never corrupt internal state or throw an exception — it simply returns 0 bytes consumed. after all bytes have been fed piecemeal, re-encoding the decoded header must reproduce the original byte sequence exactly (incremental_decode_stable=true). this is the same streaming/incremental decode behavior called out in Feature 2 of start.md: the decoder consumes bytes in growing chunks, reports bytes_consumed per call, and suspends without error on partial input, as specified in feature2_streaming_decode.json.

on the “what does an unbuilt header look like” question, let’s make that explicit: a newly created, unpopulated header must report message_id=absent, header_nonce=absent, and header_tag=absent. those three optional identity and authentication fields need to be explicitly absent, not null, not zero, not empty bytes, until the header is either fully constructed or decoded from bytes.

and two guardrails we should be strict about. first, the encryption context byte length is stored in an unsigned 16-bit field, so the maximum serialized context size is 65535 bytes. if the context exceeds that, the encoder must fail with error=encryption_context_too_large and max=65535 on the next line. it must not silently truncate the length field. second, if someone tries to serialize a header without the auth pieces in place, that should fail immediately: serializing a header that is missing either its header nonce or its header authentication tag must fail with error=missing_header_authentication followed by missing=nonce or missing=tag, whichever is absent. both have to be present before serialization is allowed, and we should never emit an unprotected header.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception { /* reads JSON line from stdin, dispatches to codec adapter, writes line-oriented text to stdout */ }

trigger_keywords: which fields are validated during decode
what byte values are rejected as malformed
decode-time field validation rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During decode, the following fixed fields are validated: version (must equal the current format version; 0 is invalid), type (must be a known object type; 0 is invalid), algorithm_id (must be a known algorithm; 0 is invalid), content_type (must be single_block or frame; byte value 10 or any unknown byte is invalid), reserved_field (must be zero; any non-zero value is invalid), nonce_length (must be non-negative), and frame_length (must be non-negative). Each violation must produce error=malformed_ciphertext_header followed by field=<field_name>.

trigger_keywords: what should decode do when given null or absent input
decoder behavior on empty byte source
polling with no data available
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a fresh decoder is asked to consume a null or absent byte source, it must be a no-op: it consumes zero bytes and outputs bytes_consumed=0 with no error. This allows a streaming caller to poll before data has arrived without any special-case handling.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be organized across files
what belongs in the adapter versus the core codec
should JSON parsing be inside the header class
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codec must be split into distinct logical units: (1) wire-format encoding/decoding logic, (2) field validation logic, and (3) the I/O adapter. The core codec must have zero knowledge of JSON or stdin/stdout. Only the adapter translates a JSON command into calls on the core type and renders results as line-oriented text. Do not collapse everything into a single god file.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for decoded structural fields
how are results printed to stdout
line-oriented text contract format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All output is line-oriented text, one key=value pair per line, ending with a newline. Standard decode/encode output includes: serialized_length, round_trip_stable or incremental_decode_stable (true/false), version, content_type, frame_length, nonce_length, encryption_context_length, and encrypted_key_blob_count. Error output is error=<error_code> followed by a detail line (e.g., field=, missing=, max=). Each line ends with \n and the full output ends with a trailing \n.

trigger_keywords: what is the nonce length value
how long is the header nonce
what is the tag size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The header nonce is 12 bytes (nonce_length=12) and the header authentication tag is 16 bytes. These are fixed constants for the format. The nonce_length field in the decoded output must always report 12 for a valid header.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided

show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values

what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'encode_round_trip', 'streaming_decode', 'decode_metadata', 'decode_empty', 'new_header_state', 'encode_oversized_context', 'encode_missing_auth', 'decode_corrupt_field'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: look at how the login module handled the compatibility validation last time for reference on the error reporting style
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The field-level validation error format used in Feature 7 (feature7_decode_field_validation.json): error=malformed_ciphertext_header on one line, field=<name> on the next line, matching the neutral language-agnostic error contract throughout the PRD

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the wire-format spec. Check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete examples of expected inputs and outputs for each scenario.

feature1_round_trip_encoding.json.description: Encode a complete message header (version, type, algorithm, message id, encryption context, one key blob, content type, reserved field, nonce length, frame length, header nonce, header tag) into its binary wire form, then decode the bytes back into a header and re-encode it. The two byte sequences must be identical, and the decoded structural fields must match what was supplied. The serialized length is fully determined by the field sizes (fixed-size fields plus the encryption-context length plus the key-blob length plus nonce and tag), so it is stable across runs even though the internal message id is random.

cases.expected_output:
serialized_length=143
round_trip_stable=true
version=1
content_type=frame
frame_length=4096
nonce_length=12
encryption_context_length=30
encrypted_key_blob_count=1

serialized_length=113
round_trip_stable=true
version=1
content_type=single_block
frame_length=0
nonce_length=12
encryption_context_length=0
encrypted_key_blob_count=1

feature2_streaming_decode.json.description: Decode a header from a stream where the bytes arrive incrementally, one growing chunk at a time. The decoder must support partial parsing: it consumes whatever complete fields it can from the bytes available so far, reports how many bytes it consumed, and resumes when more bytes arrive, without losing or corrupting any field. After the full header has been consumed in this piecewise manner, re-encoding it must reproduce the original byte sequence exactly.

cases.expected_output:
serialized_length=153
incremental_decode_stable=true
version=1
content_type=frame
frame_length=4096
nonce_length=12
encryption_context_length=40
encrypted_key_blob_count=1

feature3_decode_metadata.json.description: After decoding a header from its bytes, the recovered encryption-context length must equal the byte length of the serialized encryption context that was originally embedded, and the recovered key-blob count must equal the number of key blobs that were embedded. This verifies that the length and count fields survive an encode/decode round trip and report the true sizes of the variable-length sections.

feature4_decode_null_input.json.description: Asking a fresh decoder to consume a null/absent byte source must be a no-op: it consumes zero bytes and reports that, rather than failing. This lets a streaming caller poll for input that has not yet arrived.

feature5_empty_header_state.json.description: A newly created, empty header that has not yet been populated or decoded must report all of its optional identity/authentication fields as absent: the message id, the header nonce, and the header tag. This gives callers a well-defined empty state to distinguish from a fully built header.

feature6_1_oversized_context.json.description: Encoding rejects an encryption context whose serialized size exceeds the maximum value of an unsigned 16-bit length field (65535 bytes). Constructing a header with an over-large context must fail with a neutral too-large error that reports the maximum permitted size, rather than silently truncating the length field.

feature6_2_missing_authentication.json.description: The full header serialization includes a header nonce and a header authentication tag that protect the integrity of the preceding fields. Serializing a header while either of these two authentication values is still unset must fail with a neutral missing-authentication error that names which value is missing, rather than producing an unprotected header.

feature7_decode_field_validation.json.description: When decoding a header, every fixed structural field is validated against the format's allowed values. Encode a valid header, overwrite one field in the byte stream with an out-of-range value, then decode. Each corruption must be rejected with a neutral malformed-header error that names the offending field. Covered fields: the format version byte (must equal the current version), the object type byte (must be a known type), the algorithm identifier (must be a known algorithm), the content-type byte (must be single-block or framed), the reserved field (must be zero), the nonce-length byte (must be non-negative), and the frame-length integer (must be non-negative).
